author: @baekbeomchoi6716
text:
Hey Robert, thanks for your video. 
It really was helpful. 

I just have 2 quick questions since my listening skill is not perfect. 

1. You wash off the sand in WHAT 10% solution? 
    (I repeatedly tried to listen to the word but can't get it)

2. Could you please briefly write how to apply Activated Carbon to stop the GO oxidizing? 

Look forward to your reply.

replies.author: @ThinkingandTinkering
replies.text: HCl - what activated carbon?

replies.author: @baekbeomchoi6716
replies.text:
Thanks for your reply Rob, 

My 2nd question is at 1:00 of your video. You said in order to stop the GO oxidizing, do something with Activated Carbon. I'm not sure what you are exactly talking about. Could you please reply on this? 

Thanks in advance.

replies.author: @ThinkingandTinkering
replies.text: you want to dry the sand but not reduce the GO so a coating on the top creates an inert atmosphere - you could use anything - if you have a lab oven use a flush of nitrogen or argon, you could also use charcoal bricks - anything to reduce the possibility of the GO being reduced

replies.author: @baekbeomchoi6716
replies.text: +Robert Murray-Smith thank you very much! I appreciate that.

replies.author: @ThinkingandTinkering
replies.text: no worries mate

author: @FoxxRoscoe
text:
May I ask you Robert, if it would be possible to use sugar method creating graphene coating, and alter it back into a graphine oxide?
I am studying materials engineering and am experimenting on fly ash. I would like to know, if you think supersand would perform well  when mixed with heavy metal induced ash, so the end product would be reduced heavy metal loads of ash. There is alot of ash that cannot be recycled because of too high heavy metal concentrations and this means in the end the heavy metals end up back in the ground and in waters. (Sorry about my Englsih, it is obiously not my native language) :).

I am also open for suggestions for adding compounds to the graphene oxide ie. Silver nitrate etc. Any suggestions in mind, if so, I can report back to you on results as soon as they are finished :).

replies.author: @ThinkingandTinkering
replies.text: +Fox Roscoe I think what you suggest is more than do-able. You would have to be careful re oxidation conditions for the grapehe coated sand or you will over oxidise. There are a lot of reports in the literature re silver coating graphene and graphene oxide because of the anti microbial effect and it's use in waste water remediation - just scan google scholar

author: @dhawans
text: I see your videos on Ultra Sand (graphene coated) and Super Sand (GO coated). Can both be used effectively and what specifically is the difference? Have you done any comparisons at similar coated loadings (gms C/gms Sand)? Thanks in advance for your help.

replies.author: @ThinkingandTinkering
replies.text: +Sandeep Dhawan I take it from your question you plan on doing this and i suggest you do - let me know what you find out

replies.author: @dhawans
replies.text: +Robert Murray-Smith Thanks, infact my question was based more on curiosity, since the UltraSand seemed simpler. I am however trying to replicate making GO. 1) Can you please help me with identifying a starting point for how many gms of GO I need to coat say 100 gms of sand to make an effective water filtration setup. 2) How does one know that you have made GO-is there a simple "household" test or do I need to rely on Spectrospcopy. BTW I do like your videos, they are simple and to the point-Thank You.

replies.author: @ThinkingandTinkering
replies.text: +Sandeep Dhawan If you are planning on doing this your best bet is to do a little reading first - I can tell you haven't done enough because of your questions. The weight of GO is very small less than a gram for 100g of sand but exact percentages you need to experiment with. The only sure fire test is Raman

text: You can make a pretty concentrated silver nanoparticulate solution with a 9 volt battery and a couple of pieces of pure silver wire.  If you evaporated that down......

Perhaps I am missing something.

Clown Whisper

author: @ClownWhisper
text:
Brilliant!!!

At the risk of appearing as a nut job, Im going to share something with you.
I have been drinking Silver nanoparticles for about 5 weeks now, and my health has improved dramatically.
I no longer have difficulty in breathing, and infection that I have been fighting for 8 years is all but gone.  My neuropathy is almost completely gone and I am starting to be able to do things once again for myself.  When you mention adding AG to the sand at the very instant that it was running though my mind I had to share this.

I have had zero luck with western medicine and out of pure deportation I decided to give it a go.  I still have a major underlying condition but at least my immune system does not have to fight with this other crap!

I literally could not leave the house without an inhaler, a month ago.  Now I haven't used my inhaler more then 3 times in the past 2 weeks! 

Your simple idea would make an excellent product for the survivalist crowd.  You could make cartridges with sand and literally clean and disinfect stagnant foul water.

There is not a pathogen on earth that can survive in the presents of silver.

replies.author: @ThinkingandTinkering
replies.text: I have heard of the properties of colloidal silver mate and thought about it - but thanks for the thought

replies.author: @ClownWhisper
replies.text:
yeah its an interesting concept for sure.

This whole sand project has endless commercial possibilities....

replies.author: @ThinkingandTinkering
replies.text: Clown Whisper indeed

replies.author: @ThinkingandTinkering
replies.text: i have heard silver nanoparticles do this and i know of the antibacterial effects of silver - but in a form like this it would represent a food product and the legislation surrounding such products and their manufacture is a nightmare - not something I want to be involved with mate
